text: The camera port is positioned three centimeters above the umbilicus, followed by the placement of the right robotic port.

The surgical team places the left robotic port ten centimeters to the left and the right robotic port eleven centimeters to the right at the same level as the umbilical. An air seal port is positioned three centimeters above the anterior right spine, and a mirrored fifty millimeter port is placed three centimeters above the left anterior spine using a port-in-port technique.
The surgeon utilizes the fifty millimeter port for inserting an endocatch device to retrieve the specimen and performs stapling for the posterior neobladder construction.
An additional 12mm port is positioned between the right robotic port and the camera port to facilitate surgical assistance during the cystectomy.
The surgeon observes two dilated ureters on the right side, noting their confluence at the level of the bladder due to the advanced tumor.
The surgeon prepares to remove the uterus and ovaries, noting the parallel orientation of the two dilated ureters on the right side due to the advanced tumor.
The surgeon dissects on the left side, progressing close to the bladder, with a visible clip applied to secure the dissected area.
The surgeon begins to take down the sigmoid colon on the left side, using robotic instruments visible through the surgical ports.

The surgeon carefully approaches the tumor located on the right side, near the meatus, during the cystectomy procedure, ensuring not to encroach on the tumor area as preparations are made for constructing a neobladder.
The surgeon locates the ureter on the left side, which appears smaller and more normal compared to the right side.

The surgeon carefully navigates near the ureter, aiming to approach close to the bladder. The internal iliac artery and external common iliac artery are visible in the surgical field, indicating the region for future lymph node dissection.
The surgeon observes the ureter on the right side, noting its smaller size due to obstruction, while carefully maintaining distance to preserve circulation.
The surgeon navigates around the female bladder, noting its lateral position compared to the male bladder, and exercises caution during dissection.
The surgeon approaches the broad ligament, aiming to open it while the ureter is released and visible on this side.
The surgeon clips the left ureter, placing a clip with a suture to facilitate lifting. The artery is visible entering the surgical field.
The surgeon begins the cystectomy procedure, preparing to remove the uterus as part of the non-organ sparing approach.

The surgeon identifies the location where a sponge is descending and prepares to make an incision in the vaginal area for orientation. The ureters are visible, with one positioned lower and the other higher in the surgical field.

The surgeon identifies the bladder's location and creates an opening in the surrounding tissue to establish an anatomical reference point for the cystectomy. A sponge is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon begins the cystectomy on the right side, using a ligature to secure the ovarian vessels. A hole has been created for anatomical orientation, serving as an aiming point during the procedure.

The surgeon uses a ligature to take the ovarian vessels, with the bladder ureter visible nearby. A hole is present in the vagina, and the lateral pedicle is being taken down, moving laterally to the umbilical ligament.
The surgeon identifies the umbilical ligament and moves laterally, revealing a medial hernia near the bone, which is uncommon in females.
The surgeon examines the hernia, noting the presence of two ureters and the unusual appearance of the hernia. Efforts are made to manipulate the hernia to make it more visible.

The surgeon carefully dissects laterally towards the endopelvic fascia, ensuring the bladder remains partially attached at the top to prevent it from obstructing the surgical field. The pelvic bone and pelvic fascia become visible as the dissection progresses.
The obturator fossa is visible, with the obturator nerve identifiable within it. The nerve is clearly seen in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a ligature to divide the round ligament, preparing to manipulate it further in the surgical field.
The surgeon follows the umbilical ligament as it emerges internally, demonstrating its anatomical path within the surgical field.

The surgeon places a clip on the umbilical ligament and the superior vesicle artery to secure them during dissection. The internal iliac artery is visible, along with various branches, including the superior vesicle artery. The surgeon uses a ligature to simultaneously clip the umbilical ligament and the superior vesicle artery.
The surgeon identifies the ureter and applies a ligature to secure it in place.
The surgeon positions the ligature around the ureter, which is clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon manipulates the uterus, attempting to elevate it within the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a ligature to approach the opening of the vagina, with a visible sponge and sponge stick in the surgical field. The surgeon moves laterally during the procedure.

The surgeon identifies large veins in the surgical field, likely related to the patient's tumor. A ligature is applied to these veins, and clips are placed to secure them, with careful lateral adjustment to ensure proper positioning.
The surgeon cuts a small amount of fat to clear the view for the camera, aiming to navigate around the surrounding structures.
The surgeon applies clips to large veins in the surgical field, followed by the use of a ligature device to secure the vessels.
The surgeon requests another Hem-o-lok clip while approaching the vaginal area. The vagina is opened, and a clip is applied to secure the tissue.
The surgeon identifies a vein near the vagina and proceeds to divide it, focusing on the lateral side of the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully navigates the lateral side of the vagina, avoiding proximity to the bladder due to the presence of a tumor, and lifts the tissue for oncological reasons.
The surgeon moves laterally to the umbilical ligament on the left side, which is considered better for tumor management. The dissection reveals the bone and begins to expose the bladder.

The surgeon follows the umbilical ligament, performing dissection similar to the opposite side. The obturator fossa is visible, along with the obturator nerve and artery. The surgeon releases tissue on this side, preparing to show the position of the pudendal nerves.
The surgeon positions the ligature to take a structure near the umbilical ligaments, which are visible on this side. The first artery is identified in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies veins in the surgical field and prepares to apply a clip, followed by the use of a ligature for securing the vessels.
The surgeon exposes the uterus and adjacent vagina, preparing to apply a ligature without descending towards the veins.
The surgeon uses the fourth arm to adjust the view, revealing the vagina and ureter. Important nerves are visible running laterally into the vagina, which need to be preserved during the procedure.
The surgeon places a clip on a visible structure, preparing for ligation in the surgical field.

The surgeon lifts the bladder, revealing the surrounding neurovascular bundle, including the pudendal nerve, which is positioned similarly to the male prostate. The bladder is elevated to spare as much tissue as possible.
The surgeon lifts the tissue to expose the vagina, ensuring not to descend too far laterally during the dissection.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the tissue to avoid opening the vagina on the left side, ensuring that the dissection remains above the important nerves. The visual shows the difference in approach between the left and right sides, with the left side dissection following the lateral surface of the vagina while the right side is positioned lower.

The surgeon identifies the plane on the side to move the vagina down, using a ligature to facilitate the process. The surgical approach involves moving structures closer to the midline, contrasting with typical female cystectomy techniques.

The surgeon takes the bladder down, aiming to visualize the meatus clearly for a precise urethral dissection. The urachus is divided, and the peritoneum is opened, repeating the procedure on the left side.

The surgeon observes the anatomical differences between the right and left sides, noting the more standard anatomy on the left. The assistant carefully pulls on the catheter to expose the urethra, revealing the bladder neck positioned below.

The surgeon manipulates the catheter, pulling it to reveal the balloon near the bladder neck on both sides. The balloon is visible as it is pulled down, demonstrating the anatomical positioning relative to the bladder neck.

The surgeon observes the balloon on the left side near the bladder neck, while on the right side, a tumor is visible adjacent to the balloon, indicating proximity to the bladder neck. The anatomical structures are carefully inspected to ensure the tumor is not approached too closely.
The surgeon assesses the margin status while the balloon remains visible on the left side near the bladder neck, indicating proximity to the tumor.
The surgeon adjusts the catheter, pulling it down to reposition it in the midline, allowing for better visualization below the tumor at the bladder neck.

The surgeon exposes the urethra and vaginal wall, preparing to dissect under the bladder neck for several centimeters. The surgical field shows the planned suture line on the vagina, with the anastomosis positioned closely, requiring careful preparation.
The surgeon carefully pulls the catheter down, revealing the tumor located on the right side, ensuring it is not too close to the catheter. The tumor's major part is visible on the side.
The surgeon cuts the vaginal wall, with visible tissue separation occurring as the incision progresses.

The surgeon performs intracorporeal robotic cystectomy, focusing on suturing while adjusting the catheter to prevent gas leakage. The catheter is clamped externally to ensure no leakage occurs during the procedure.
The surgeon is close to the anastomosis site, where the mesenteric border is visible with minimal serosa present.
The surgeon is suturing on the left side, adjusting the suture direction due to the longer studer on this side, moving the suture downwards and then to the left.

The surgeon prepares for the folding of the Studer pouch, showing both ends of the sutured segment. Using a stay suture as a reference point, the surgeon begins to close the anterior half of the pouch, aiming to create a spherical shape. The catheter is adjusted slightly during this process.

The surgeon prepares to place stents and create the Wallace plate, with half of the anterior part of the Studer pouch closed. The surgical field shows two ureters positioned below and one ureter above, facilitating an easier anastomosis at the upper location.

The surgeon opens the surgical field to reveal an unexpected anatomical configuration with multiple ureters. Three stents are prepared for placement due to the presence of an extra ureter. A 5-0 PDS suture is used with a needle driver to begin suturing from the outside in.
The surgeon adjusts the lighting to improve visibility in the surgical field, then proceeds to suture side to side using a PDS 5-0 suture.
The surgeon prepares to open the chimney, ensuring a non-touch technique is used to avoid contact with the ureter while handling the needle.
The surgeon frees the tissue to improve visibility of the anatomical structures before performing the anastomosis. The serosa is exposed, and the needle driver is prepared for use.

The surgeon positions the needle driver in the right hand and introduces the stents into the midline. The chimney from the previous opening is visible, and the catheter is inserted further inside. The catheter is filled with ten cc's and pulled down.
The surgeon fills the catheter and pulls it down, preparing for the anastomosis. The stance is positioned, and the surgical team discusses using a simple G stent for controlled output.
The surgeon places two similar catheters on both sides and removes the guide wire. They identify the ureter with a stent and note that the other ureter does not have a stent.
The surgeon adjusts the position of the stent, ensuring it is correctly aligned with the same ureter on the right side.
The surgeon uses a barbed suture to perform delicate stitching, preferring smaller barbs for precision. The suture is maneuvered from the outside in, facilitating easier handling.
The surgeon requests scissors and proceeds to cut the ureter while preserving adjacent tissue, allowing the ureters to be lifted for further manipulation.
The surgeon holds the ureters, flipping them to the opposite side, allowing for clear visualization of the ureter on this side.
The surgeon observes a loose area in the surgical field and requests suction to clear the view. An extra stitch is needed to address the issue, and additional suture material is prepared.
The surgeon requests scissors to be placed in their right hand, indicating the final stages of the procedure.
The surgeon cuts the left ureter while using a needle driver to place a couple of stitches in the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon completes the procedure by cutting the remaining suture and applying a V-lock suture to secure the area, ensuring the ureters are properly positioned.
The surgeon cuts close to the urethra, identifying the small urethra on the left and the double urethra on the right for pathology. The final step involves closing the last part of the neobladder.
The surgeon sutures the stent in place, preparing a syringe to check for watertight integrity. An assistant uses suction to clear the area.
The surgeon checks the neobladder for watertight integrity by attempting to inflate it, observing for any potential leaks.
